title: Atopic Eczema
oneLiner: Chronic pruritic inflammatory skin disease with defective barrier, Th2 skew, and childhood onset; high morbidity, infectious risk, and psychosocial impact.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 6-month-old infant presents with a red, scaly, weeping rash on the cheeks, chin, and extensor surfaces of the arms and legs. The mother reports intense itching — the baby rubs against the cot sheets, can’t sleep, and has had three similar flares that improved briefly with over‑the‑counter hydrocortisone. The infant’s older sibling has asthma, and the mother has hayfever. On exam, the skin feels dry and rough (“xerosis”) with tiny papules and excoriations; flexural areas (antecubital and popliteal fossae) are now starting to show lichenification.

**Pattern clues dissection:**
- **Age < 2 years / flexural involvement** → infantile eczema pattern; later in childhood flexural becomes dominant (antecubital, popliteal, neck).
- **Intense pruritus** → hallmark; scratching drives the itch‑scratch cycle and leads to lichenification.
- **Family atopy** (asthma, rhinitis, eczema) → strong genetic component (filaggrin mutations, Th2 bias).
- **Xerosis** → barrier defect; skin cannot hold water.
- **Weeping / exudate** → acute inflammation with IgE‑mediated mast cell degranulation; also risk of secondary bacterial infection.
- **Response to mild topical steroid** → validates inflammatory nature; poor response suggests alternative or infection.
- **Sleep disturbance** → objective marker of severity (itch worse at night).

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Adult‑onset eczema** (often hand eczema, peri‑ocular, or nipple eczema) → mimic allergic contact dermatitis or psoriasis; check occupation (irritants), metal allergy.
- **Nummular eczema** (coin‑shaped plaques without flexural predominance) → can be misdiagnosed as tinea corporis or psoriasis.
- **Erythroderma** (total body redness, scaling, lymphadenopathy) → can mimic T‑cell lymphoma, severe psoriasis, drug eruption; requires urgent dermatology referral.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain‑language definition:**  
Atopic eczema is a chronic, relapsing inflammation of the skin driven by a defective epidermal barrier and an overactive Type 2 immune response (Th2). It typically starts in infancy and is associated with other atopic conditions (asthma, allergic rhinitis, food allergy). It is not a single disease but a syndrome with multiple genetic and environmental contributors.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Affects 10–20% of children in developed countries; up to 3% persist severely into adulthood.
- **Stakes:** Impaired quality of life (sleep, school, social stigma), risk of secondary infection (Staph aureus, HSV — eczema herpeticum can be fatal), and long‑term sequelae (growth delay in severe paediatric cases, psychosocial impact).
- **Common pitfalls:** Under‑treating with emollients, over‑using potent topical steroids on delicate areas, mistaking infected eczema for simple flare (missing impetigo or HSV), and failing to recognise eczema herpeticum early.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**Story of development:**  
The problem starts at the skin barrier. Mutations in the filaggrin gene (FLG) — present in ~30% of European‑ancestry patients — lead to defective cornified envelope, reduced natural moisturising factor, and a leaky stratum corneum. Allergens, microbes (especially Staph aureus), and irritants easily penetrate the epidermis. Antigen‑presenting cells (Langerhans cells) pick them up and migrate to lymph nodes, where they prime naïve T cells to become Th2 cells (IL‑4, IL‑13, IL‑31). This drives IgE production (via B cells) and eosinophil recruitment. Th2 cytokines suppress barrier repair further (downregulate filaggrin, loricrin), creating a vicious cycle.

**Mechanism → clinical sign:**
- **Barrier defect + Th2 inflammation** → **xerosis** (water loss) and **erythema** (vasodilation from IL‑4, histamine).
- **IL‑31** (the “itch cytokine”) → **intense pruritus** → scratching → mechanical damage → lichenification (thickened skin from chronic rubbing).
- **IgE‑mediated mast cell degranulation** → **wheal‑and‑flare, oedema, weeping** in acute flares.
- **Staph aureus colonisation** (promoted by reduced antimicrobial peptides) → **bacterial superinfection** (impetiginised eczema: honey‑coloured crusts, pustules).
- **Th2 dominance suppresses Th1** → increased susceptibility to viral infections (HSV, molluscum contagiosum).

**Connect to pattern recognition cues:**  
The infant’s cheek/extensor rash corresponds to acute barrier failure at sites of rubbing and saliva (face, extensors). The lichenified flexures in older children reflect chronic scratching. Family atopy is a proxy for shared genetic risk (filaggrin, Th2‑prone immune genes).
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Examples | Stem clue to point to cause |
|----------|----------|-----------------------------|
| **Genetic (intrinsic)** | Filaggrin mutation, chromosome 5q31 (Th2 gene cluster) | Strong family history of atopy; early‑onset severe disease; palmar hyperlinearity (from filaggrin defect) |
| **Environmental triggers — allergens** | House dust mite, pollen, pet dander, certain foods (egg, milk, peanut) in young children | Flare with season, after moving house, after eating specific food; personal or family history of food allergy |
| **Environmental triggers — irritants** | Soaps, detergents, wool, chlorinated water, excessive bathing | Flare after hand washing, swimming, or change of laundry product |
| **Microbial triggers** | *Staphylococcus aureus* superantigens, *Malassezia* yeast (head/neck eczema), HSV | Worsening after respiratory infection; weeping crusted patches (impetigo); punched‑out vesicles (herpeticum) |
| **Psychogenic / stress** | Emotional stress, sleep deprivation | Flare during exams, divorce, moving; patient reports “stress brings it out” |
| **Climate** | Cold dry air (winter), low humidity, heat and sweating | Seasonal flares (winter for xerosis, summer for sweating) |
| **Hormonal** | Pregnancy, premenstrual | Worsening during menses or pregnancy; often first‑time eczema in pregnant woman |

**Most common trigger:** The majority of patients have an intrinsic genetic predisposition (filaggrin mutation) and will flare with **Staph aureus overgrowth** and/or **irritant exposure**. In infants, food allergens (egg, milk) can be a trigger, but routine elimination diets are discouraged without evidence of specific IgE.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (clinical — no mandatory test):**  
UK Working Party’s diagnostic criteria (simplified):  
- **Pruritus** (essential)  
- **Typical morphology and distribution** (flexural lichenification in adults, facial/extensor in infants)  
- **Chronic/relapsing course**  
- **Personal or first‑degree family history of atopy**

**Tests (ordered selectively):**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Serum total IgE** | Elevated in ~80% with active disease | Support diagnosis; track severity | > 100 IU/mL suggestive; normal does not rule out (intrinsic eczema) |
| **Specific IgE / RAST** (e.g., egg, milk, dust mite) | Level of sensitisation | Identify triggers if moderate‑to‑severe and history suggests | Positive ≠ clinical allergy; must correlate with history |
| **Skin prick test** | Immediate wheal‑and‑flare | Fast, cheap; done in allergy clinic | > 3 mm diameter = positive; false‑positive rare but possible |
| **Patch test** | Delayed hypersensitivity (contact allergens) | Suspect allergic contact dermatitis (hand eczema, treatment‑resistant) | Positive patch = allergic contact dermatitis superimposed |
| **Skin swab (bacterial, viral)** | Staph aureus, Streptococcus, HSV | Weeping/crusted lesions, fever, punched‑out vesicles | Staph aureus → impetiginised eczema; HSV → eczema herpeticum (emergency) |
| **Skin biopsy** | Spongiosis (epidermal oedema), lymphocytic exocytosis | Atypical presentation (adult‑onset, erythroderma, rule out lymphoma) | Spongiosis + lymphocytic infiltrate supporting eczema; no Pautrier microabscesses (rule out mycosis fungoides) |

**Single finding that confirms:** No single test confirms; **typical distribution + pruritus + xerosis + chronic course** (with or without atopic family history) is the gold standard.  
**Single finding that rules out:** Absence of pruritus essentially rules out atopic eczema — consider psoriasis, seborrhoeic dermatitis, or cutaneous T‑cell lymphoma.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour – when called to review acute flare or infection):**
- **Assess severity:** Use SCORAD (subjective + objective + extent) or simple “rule of nines” for skin involvement? Not practical in first hour. Quick: count number of areas (face, trunk, arms, legs, neck), acute signs (oedema, weeping, crusting), fever, lymphadenopathy.
- **If suspected eczema herpeticum** (punched‑out vesicles, systemic malaise, fever):  
  - Start **IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h** (adjusted for renal function)  
  - Call dermatology and infection control  
  - Do Tzanck smear / viral PCR from unroofed vesicle
- **If suspected impetiginised eczema** (honey‑yellow crusts, pustules, widespread):  
  - Start **flucloxacillin 500 mg PO QDS** (or cephalexin if penicillin allergic) for 7 days  
  - Consider MRSA risk → send swab and start clindamycin 300 mg PO QDS empirically
- **For severe acute itch that is preventing medication application:**  
  - Give a sedating antihistamine (e.g., **chlorphenamine 4 mg PO stat**) to break the itch‑scratch cycle and allow emollient + topical steroid to be applied.

**Ward (24–72 hours – stabilising and step‑up):**
- **Emollients** — first line, every time.  
  - Prescribe a **greasy emollient** (e.g., 50:50 white soft paraffin/liquid paraffin) or **aqueous cream** (avoid as leave‑on — can sting; better as soap substitute).  
  - Apply **liberally**, at least twice daily, immediately after bathing (“soak and seal”).
- **Topical corticosteroids:**  
  - Face, neck, groin, axillae → **hydrocortisone 1% cream BD** (mild potency) for up to 7 days.  
  - Body, limbs → **betamethasone valerate 0.1% cream BD** (moderate potency) for 7–10 days, then step down.  
  - Thick lichenified plaques → **clobetasone butyrate 0.05%** or **mometasone furoate 0.1%** (potent) for 7–10 days.  
  - **Rule:** Use enough (fingertip unit: 1 FTU = 0.5 g, covers 2 palm areas). Taper frequency to BD then OD. Do not abruptly stop — risk of rebound.
- **Wet wrap therapy** (for severe flares): apply topical steroid then a wet layer of tubular bandage, then a dry layer. Change every 12 h. Use for 2–5 days only.  
- **Sedating antihistamines** (night): promethazine 10–20 mg PO nocte, or chlorphenamine 4 mg PO QDS PRN.  
- **If severe, hospitalised, no response:**  
  - Consider **oral prednisolone** (0.5–1 mg/kg/day for 5–7 days only — rapid taper; steroid‑sparing agents needed before taper completes).  
  - Dermatology consult for **UV phototherapy** (narrowband UVB) or **systemic immunosuppressants** (ciclosporin 2.5–5 mg/kg/day PO divided BD; monitor BP and renal function).

**Long‑term (prevent relapse, manage chronic disease):**
- **Continuous emollient use** — the cornerstone.  
- **Proactive topical steroid pulse** (e.g., once‑daily moderate potency twice weekly to previously affected areas — “weekend therapy”).  
- **Identify and avoid triggers** — dust mite covers, hypoallergenic soap, cotton clothing, avoid wool.
- **Educate:** Itch‑scratch cycle; apply steroid first on active lesions, then emollient over entire body.
- **Treat co‑existing conditions:** topical tacrolimus 0.03–0.1% BD for sensitive areas (face, eyelids) — no steroid atrophy.  
- **Severe refractory cases:**  
  - **Azathioprine** (1–3 mg/kg/day PO; check TPMT), **methotrexate** (10–15 mg once weekly), **ciclosporin** (2.5–5 mg/kg/day), or **dupilumab** (IL‑4Rα antibody — 300 mg SC fortnightly).  
  - Refer to dermatology.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- Avoid prolonged potent steroids on face, groin, axillae → skin atrophy, striae, rosacea, glaucoma (periocular).  
- Do not over‑use antihistamines in children — daytime sedation affects school.  
- Do not routinely use **emollient alone** on active/flaring eczema — must use topical steroid.  
- Do not use **antibiotics without signs of infection** — no evidence for prophylactic antibiotics.  
- Do not stop systemic steroids abruptly — always taper.  
- Do not rely on “natural” or herbal creams (may contain undisclosed potent steroids).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Atopic Eczema | Seborrhoeic Dermatitis | Psoriasis (plaque) | Allergic Contact Dermatitis |
|---------|---------------|------------------------|--------------------|----------------------------|
| **Itch** | Intense, primary | Mild or absent (unless scalp) | Mild to moderate | Intense, often burning |
| **Distribution** | Flexures (antecubital, popliteal), face, neck; infant extensors | Scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, central chest (seborrhoeic areas) | Extensor surfaces (knees, elbows), scalp, lower back; nail pitting | Site of allergen exposure (hands, face, feet) — linear shape if plant |
| **Morphology** | Erythema, weeping, lichenification, xerosis | Yellow‑greasy scales on red base (cradle cap); no weeping | Well‑defined, silvery‑scaly plaques, Koebner phenomenon; no weeping | Vesicles/bullae early, weeping, crusting; well‑demarcated |
| **Family history** | Atopy (eczema, asthma, hay fever) | Often negative; can be familial | Psoriasis in 30% | Negative (but personal hx of metal allergy) |
| **Laboratory** | High IgE (80%), eosinophilia, positive RAST | Normal IgE; may have *Malassezia* antibodies | Normal IgE; occasionally high uric acid; no specific marker | Patch test positive to a specific allergen |
| **Response to steroids** | Good initially, rapid relapse if tapered | Good; also responds to antifungals (ketoconazole) | Slow improvement; often needs vitamin D analogue ± steroid | Excellent; resolves once allergen removed |
| **Key differentiator** | Pruritus + flexural distribution + atopy history | Greasy scale in seborrhoeic areas, negative atopy history | Silvery scale, extensor plaques, nail pits, psoriatic arthritis | Linear pattern, positive patch test, history of exposure to new product |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation | Key signs | Action |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|--------|
| **Impetiginised eczema** (secondary Staph aureus infection) | Worsening erythema, weeping, honey‑coloured crusts, pustules; may have fever | CRP ↑, swab grows Staph | Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS × 7 days; if MRSA → clindamycin |
| **Eczema herpeticum (Kaposi varicelliform eruption)** | Widespread painful, punched‑out vesicles (roof often absent), fever, lymphadenopathy; can involve any skin | Malaise, eye involvement (conjunctivitis, keratitis), rapid spread over hours to days | IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h; dermatology/ophthalmology review; barrier nursing |
| **Erythroderma** (total body > 90% involved) | Red, scaly, hot, shivering, fluid loss, high‑output cardiac failure, hypoalbuminaemia | Fever, tachycardia, peripheral oedema, generalised lymphadenopathy | Urgent admission, IV fluids, emollients, topical mild steroids, dermatology review; rule out lymphoma |
| **Lichenification (chronic)** | Thickened, leathery skin with exaggerated skin markings; persists even after inflammation settles | Hyperpigmentation in darker skin types | Topical potent steroid pulse; need patient education to stop scratching |
| **Growth retardation (paediatric)** | Falling off growth centiles, delayed puberty | Height/weight < 2nd centile, bone age delay | Optimise skin care, consider systemic therapy (e.g., ciclosporin), refer to paediatric endocrinology |
| **Psychosocial** | Poor self‑image, school absence, social withdrawal | Sleep disturbance, depression/anxiety screens positive | Counselling, support groups, treat itch aggressively; refer to mental health |

**Late‑presentation patterns (exam stems):**  
- A child with untreated severe atopic eczema develops sudden widespread weepy vesicles and high fever — *that’s eczema herpeticum*.  
- An adult with long‑standing eczema presents with exfoliative dermatitis, generalised lymphadenopathy, and weight loss — *could be erythrodermic lymphoma (Sézary syndrome) — always biopsy.*  
- A patient with moderate eczema who over‑uses potent steroids on the face presents with perioral dermatitis and steroid withdrawal erythema — *manage with antibiotic creams (metronidazole) and taper steroid.*
sections.9. High‑Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
- **S.A.F.E. Management** (initial approach):  
  - **S** — Soap substitutes (emollient cleanser)  
  - **A** — Anti‑inflammatory (topical steroid)  
  - **F** — Frequent emollient (moisturise 2–3 times daily)  
  - **E** — Education (itch‑scratch, trigger avoidance)
- **DRY SKIN** (typical findings):  
  - **D** Dryness (xerosis)  
  - **R** Redness (erythema)  
  - **Y** You itch (pruritus)  
  - **S** Scaling (fine, not silvery)  
  - **K** Keep it in the flexures  
  - **I** It’s chronic  
  - **N** No silvery scale (vs psoriasis)

**Clinical pearls (pattern‑recognition cues):**
1. **“Somersault sign”** in infants: The baby rubs face on the bed → excoriation on cheeks and chin; think atopic eczema first.
2. **Flexural lichenification in an adult with no previous history** → consider contact dermatitis (e.g., nickel allergy from belt buckle) or atopic eczema that was mild in childhood.
3. **Pustules on a background of eczema** = infected until proven otherwise — swab and treat.
4. **“Eyelid eczema”** in an adult female → patch test for cosmetics (especially thioglycolates in hair dye) as well as atopic.
5. **Palmar / plantar hyperlinearity** + eczema in a child → think filaggrin mutation — ask about ichthyosis vulgaris.
6. **Erythroderma + personal history of eczema** → still needs biopsy: Sézary syndrome can mimic severe atopic eczema.
7. **“Weekend therapy” (twice‑weekly proactive steroid)** reduces flares by 50% in moderate eczema — always teach this.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Mistaking nummular eczema for tinea corporis — both have annular plaques, but tinea has raised scaly border and central clearing; scraping for KOH.
- Using **aqueous cream** as a leave‑on moisturiser — it contains sodium lauryl sulphate (irritant) and can sting; better as a soap substitute.
- Forgetting to treat **Staph aureus** when exposed dermatitis is present — systemic antibiotics not always needed (bacterial load reduced by steroid + antibiotics in ointment? But if weeping, give oral).
- Prescribing **sedating antihistamines** daytime — causes sedation; use non‑sedating (cetirizine, loratadine) for day, sedating at night.
- Starting **oral steroids** without a plan for steroid‑sparing agent — rebound flare is almost guaranteed.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior (dermatology registrar or consultant):**
- Suspected **eczema herpeticum** — needs IV aciclovir and expert review.
- **Erythroderma** (widespread redness, scaling, systemic symptoms) — needs admission, skin biopsy, and ruling out lymphoma.
- **Severe facial eczema with periorbital involvement** — risk of eye involvement (keratitis, cataracts from steroids); ophthalmology review.
- **Failure of topical therapy** (potent steroid + emollient + wet wraps for 5–7 days no response) — need phototherapy or systemic agents.
- **Severe childhood eczema with growth failure** — paediatric dermatology and endocrinology input.
- **Worsening on systemic immunosuppressants** (e.g., rising creatinine on ciclosporin, leucopenia on azathioprine) — urgent dose adjustment / cessation.

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- **Sepsis** secondary to infected eczema (fever, hypotension, tachycardia, lactate > 2 mmol/L, altered mental state) — start IV antibiotics and fluids; consult ICU for vasopressors if septic shock.
- **Eczema herpeticum with disseminated herpes** (pneumonitis, hepatitis, encephalitis) — ICU for organ support.
- **Anaphylaxis** (rare — from topical antibiotic/NSAID allergy in a patient with eczema) — IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg, call crash team.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (or any single parameter scoring 3): escalate to senior clinician.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** (sepsis): start IV antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, consider ICU.
- **GCS

## Atopic Eczema
**One-liner:** Chronic pruritic inflammatory skin disease with defective barrier, Th2 skew, and childhood onset; high morbidity, infectious risk, and psychosocial impact.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic vignette:**  
A 6-month-old infant presents with a red, scaly, weeping rash on the cheeks, chin, and extensor surfaces of the arms and legs. The mother reports intense itching — the baby rubs against the cot sheets, can’t sleep, and has had three similar flares that improved briefly with over‑the‑counter hydrocortisone. The infant’s older sibling has asthma, and the mother has hayfever. On exam, the skin feels dry and rough (“xerosis”) with tiny papules and excoriations; flexural areas (antecubital and popliteal fossae) are now starting to show lichenification.

**Pattern clues dissection:**
- **Age < 2 years / flexural involvement** → infantile eczema pattern; later in childhood flexural becomes dominant (antecubital, popliteal, neck).
- **Intense pruritus** → hallmark; scratching drives the itch‑scratch cycle and leads to lichenification.
- **Family atopy** (asthma, rhinitis, eczema) → strong genetic component (filaggrin mutations, Th2 bias).
- **Xerosis** → barrier defect; skin cannot hold water.
- **Weeping / exudate** → acute inflammation with IgE‑mediated mast cell degranulation; also risk of secondary bacterial infection.
- **Response to mild topical steroid** → validates inflammatory nature; poor response suggests alternative or infection.
- **Sleep disturbance** → objective marker of severity (itch worse at night).

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Adult‑onset eczema** (often hand eczema, peri‑ocular, or nipple eczema) → mimic allergic contact dermatitis or psoriasis; check occupation (irritants), metal allergy.
- **Nummular eczema** (coin‑shaped plaques without flexural predominance) → can be misdiagnosed as tinea corporis or psoriasis.
- **Erythroderma** (total body redness, scaling, lymphadenopathy) → can mimic T‑cell lymphoma, severe psoriasis, drug eruption; requires urgent dermatology referral.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Plain‑language definition:**  
Atopic eczema is a chronic, relapsing inflammation of the skin driven by a defective epidermal barrier and an overactive Type 2 immune response (Th2). It typically starts in infancy and is associated with other atopic conditions (asthma, allergic rhinitis, food allergy). It is not a single disease but a syndrome with multiple genetic and environmental contributors.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Affects 10–20% of children in developed countries; up to 3% persist severely into adulthood.
- **Stakes:** Impaired quality of life (sleep, school, social stigma), risk of secondary infection (Staph aureus, HSV — eczema herpeticum can be fatal), and long‑term sequelae (growth delay in severe paediatric cases, psychosocial impact).
- **Common pitfalls:** Under‑treating with emollients, over‑using potent topical steroids on delicate areas, mistaking infected eczema for simple flare (missing impetigo or HSV), and failing to recognise eczema herpeticum early.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings
**Story of development:**  
The problem starts at the skin barrier. Mutations in the filaggrin gene (FLG) — present in ~30% of European‑ancestry patients — lead to defective cornified envelope, reduced natural moisturising factor, and a leaky stratum corneum. Allergens, microbes (especially Staph aureus), and irritants easily penetrate the epidermis. Antigen‑presenting cells (Langerhans cells) pick them up and migrate to lymph nodes, where they prime naïve T cells to become Th2 cells (IL‑4, IL‑13, IL‑31). This drives IgE production (via B cells) and eosinophil recruitment. Th2 cytokines suppress barrier repair further (downregulate filaggrin, loricrin), creating a vicious cycle.

**Mechanism → clinical sign:**
- **Barrier defect + Th2 inflammation** → **xerosis** (water loss) and **erythema** (vasodilation from IL‑4, histamine).
- **IL‑31** (the “itch cytokine”) → **intense pruritus** → scratching → mechanical damage → lichenification (thickened skin from chronic rubbing).
- **IgE‑mediated mast cell degranulation** → **wheal‑and‑flare, oedema, weeping** in acute flares.
- **Staph aureus colonisation** (promoted by reduced antimicrobial peptides) → **bacterial superinfection** (impetiginised eczema: honey‑coloured crusts, pustules).
- **Th2 dominance suppresses Th1** → increased susceptibility to viral infections (HSV, molluscum contagiosum).

**Connect to pattern recognition cues:**  
The infant’s cheek/extensor rash corresponds to acute barrier failure at sites of rubbing and saliva (face, extensors). The lichenified flexures in older children reflect chronic scratching. Family atopy is a proxy for shared genetic risk (filaggrin, Th2‑prone immune genes).

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Category | Examples | Stem clue to point to cause |
|----------|----------|-----------------------------|
| **Genetic (intrinsic)** | Filaggrin mutation, chromosome 5q31 (Th2 gene cluster) | Strong family history of atopy; early‑onset severe disease; palmar hyperlinearity (from filaggrin defect) |
| **Environmental triggers — allergens** | House dust mite, pollen, pet dander, certain foods (egg, milk, peanut) in young children | Flare with season, after moving house, after eating specific food; personal or family history of food allergy |
| **Environmental triggers — irritants** | Soaps, detergents, wool, chlorinated water, excessive bathing | Flare after hand washing, swimming, or change of laundry product |
| **Microbial triggers** | *Staphylococcus aureus* superantigens, *Malassezia* yeast (head/neck eczema), HSV | Worsening after respiratory infection; weeping crusted patches (impetigo); punched‑out vesicles (herpeticum) |
| **Psychogenic / stress** | Emotional stress, sleep deprivation | Flare during exams, divorce, moving; patient reports “stress brings it out” |
| **Climate** | Cold dry air (winter), low humidity, heat and sweating | Seasonal flares (winter for xerosis, summer for sweating) |
| **Hormonal** | Pregnancy, premenstrual | Worsening during menses or pregnancy; often first‑time eczema in pregnant woman |

**Most common trigger:** The majority of patients have an intrinsic genetic predisposition (filaggrin mutation) and will flare with **Staph aureus overgrowth** and/or **irritant exposure**. In infants, food allergens (egg, milk) can be a trigger, but routine elimination diets are discouraged without evidence of specific IgE.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria (clinical — no mandatory test):**  
UK Working Party’s diagnostic criteria (simplified):  
- **Pruritus** (essential)  
- **Typical morphology and distribution** (flexural lichenification in adults, facial/extensor in infants)  
- **Chronic/relapsing course**  
- **Personal or first‑degree family history of atopy**

**Tests (ordered selectively):**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Serum total IgE** | Elevated in ~80% with active disease | Support diagnosis; track severity | > 100 IU/mL suggestive; normal does not rule out (intrinsic eczema) |
| **Specific IgE / RAST** (e.g., egg, milk, dust mite) | Level of sensitisation | Identify triggers if moderate‑to‑severe and history suggests | Positive ≠ clinical allergy; must correlate with history |
| **Skin prick test** | Immediate wheal‑and‑flare | Fast, cheap; done in allergy clinic | > 3 mm diameter = positive; false‑positive rare but possible |
| **Patch test** | Delayed hypersensitivity (contact allergens) | Suspect allergic contact dermatitis (hand eczema, treatment‑resistant) | Positive patch = allergic contact dermatitis superimposed |
| **Skin swab (bacterial, viral)** | Staph aureus, Streptococcus, HSV | Weeping/crusted lesions, fever, punched‑out vesicles | Staph aureus → impetiginised eczema; HSV → eczema herpeticum (emergency) |
| **Skin biopsy** | Spongiosis (epidermal oedema), lymphocytic exocytosis | Atypical presentation (adult‑onset, erythroderma, rule out lymphoma) | Spongiosis + lymphocytic infiltrate supporting eczema; no Pautrier microabscesses (rule out mycosis fungoides) |

**Single finding that confirms:** No single test confirms; **typical distribution + pruritus + xerosis + chronic course** (with or without atopic family history) is the gold standard.  
**Single finding that rules out:** Absence of pruritus essentially rules out atopic eczema — consider psoriasis, seborrhoeic dermatitis, or cutaneous T‑cell lymphoma.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour – when called to review acute flare or infection):**
- **Assess severity:** Use SCORAD (subjective + objective + extent) or simple “rule of nines” for skin involvement? Not practical in first hour. Quick: count number of areas (face, trunk, arms, legs, neck), acute signs (oedema, weeping, crusting), fever, lymphadenopathy.
- **If suspected eczema herpeticum** (punched‑out vesicles, systemic malaise, fever):  
  - Start **IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h** (adjusted for renal function)  
  - Call dermatology and infection control  
  - Do Tzanck smear / viral PCR from unroofed vesicle
- **If suspected impetiginised eczema** (honey‑yellow crusts, pustules, widespread):  
  - Start **flucloxacillin 500 mg PO QDS** (or cephalexin if penicillin allergic) for 7 days  
  - Consider MRSA risk → send swab and start clindamycin 300 mg PO QDS empirically
- **For severe acute itch that is preventing medication application:**  
  - Give a sedating antihistamine (e.g., **chlorphenamine 4 mg PO stat**) to break the itch‑scratch cycle and allow emollient + topical steroid to be applied.

**Ward (24–72 hours – stabilising and step‑up):**
- **Emollients** — first line, every time.  
  - Prescribe a **greasy emollient** (e.g., 50:50 white soft paraffin/liquid paraffin) or **aqueous cream** (avoid as leave‑on — can sting; better as soap substitute).  
  - Apply **liberally**, at least twice daily, immediately after bathing (“soak and seal”).
- **Topical corticosteroids:**  
  - Face, neck, groin, axillae → **hydrocortisone 1% cream BD** (mild potency) for up to 7 days.  
  - Body, limbs → **betamethasone valerate 0.1% cream BD** (moderate potency) for 7–10 days, then step down.  
  - Thick lichenified plaques → **clobetasone butyrate 0.05%** or **mometasone furoate 0.1%** (potent) for 7–10 days.  
  - **Rule:** Use enough (fingertip unit: 1 FTU = 0.5 g, covers 2 palm areas). Taper frequency to BD then OD. Do not abruptly stop — risk of rebound.
- **Wet wrap therapy** (for severe flares): apply topical steroid then a wet layer of tubular bandage, then a dry layer. Change every 12 h. Use for 2–5 days only.  
- **Sedating antihistamines** (night): promethazine 10–20 mg PO nocte, or chlorphenamine 4 mg PO QDS PRN.  
- **If severe, hospitalised, no response:**  
  - Consider **oral prednisolone** (0.5–1 mg/kg/day for 5–7 days only — rapid taper; steroid‑sparing agents needed before taper completes).  
  - Dermatology consult for **UV phototherapy** (narrowband UVB) or **systemic immunosuppressants** (ciclosporin 2.5–5 mg/kg/day PO divided BD; monitor BP and renal function).

**Long‑term (prevent relapse, manage chronic disease):**
- **Continuous emollient use** — the cornerstone.  
- **Proactive topical steroid pulse** (e.g., once‑daily moderate potency twice weekly to previously affected areas — “weekend therapy”).  
- **Identify and avoid triggers** — dust mite covers, hypoallergenic soap, cotton clothing, avoid wool.
- **Educate:** Itch‑scratch cycle; apply steroid first on active lesions, then emollient over entire body.
- **Treat co‑existing conditions:** topical tacrolimus 0.03–0.1% BD for sensitive areas (face, eyelids) — no steroid atrophy.  
- **Severe refractory cases:**  
  - **Azathioprine** (1–3 mg/kg/day PO; check TPMT), **methotrexate** (10–15 mg once weekly), **ciclosporin** (2.5–5 mg/kg/day), or **dupilumab** (IL‑4Rα antibody — 300 mg SC fortnightly).  
  - Refer to dermatology.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- Avoid prolonged potent steroids on face, groin, axillae → skin atrophy, striae, rosacea, glaucoma (periocular).  
- Do not over‑use antihistamines in children — daytime sedation affects school.  
- Do not routinely use **emollient alone** on active/flaring eczema — must use topical steroid.  
- Do not use **antibiotics without signs of infection** — no evidence for prophylactic antibiotics.  
- Do not stop systemic steroids abruptly — always taper.  
- Do not rely on “natural” or herbal creams (may contain undisclosed potent steroids).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
| Feature | Atopic Eczema | Seborrhoeic Dermatitis | Psoriasis (plaque) | Allergic Contact Dermatitis |
|---------|---------------|------------------------|--------------------|----------------------------|
| **Itch** | Intense, primary | Mild or absent (unless scalp) | Mild to moderate | Intense, often burning |
| **Distribution** | Flexures (antecubital, popliteal), face, neck; infant extensors | Scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, central chest (seborrhoeic areas) | Extensor surfaces (knees, elbows), scalp, lower back; nail pitting | Site of allergen exposure (hands, face, feet) — linear shape if plant |
| **Morphology** | Erythema, weeping, lichenification, xerosis | Yellow‑greasy scales on red base (cradle cap); no weeping | Well‑defined, silvery‑scaly plaques, Koebner phenomenon; no weeping | Vesicles/bullae early, weeping, crusting; well‑demarcated |
| **Family history** | Atopy (eczema, asthma, hay fever) | Often negative; can be familial | Psoriasis in 30% | Negative (but personal hx of metal allergy) |
| **Laboratory** | High IgE (80%), eosinophilia, positive RAST | Normal IgE; may have *Malassezia* antibodies | Normal IgE; occasionally high uric acid; no specific marker | Patch test positive to a specific allergen |
| **Response to steroids** | Good initially, rapid relapse if tapered | Good; also responds to antifungals (ketoconazole) | Slow improvement; often needs vitamin D analogue ± steroid | Excellent; resolves once allergen removed |
| **Key differentiator** | Pruritus + flexural distribution + atopy history | Greasy scale in seborrhoeic areas, negative atopy history | Silvery scale, extensor plaques, nail pits, psoriatic arthritis | Linear pattern, positive patch test, history of exposure to new product |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
| Complication | Presentation | Key signs | Action |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|--------|
| **Impetiginised eczema** (secondary Staph aureus infection) | Worsening erythema, weeping, honey‑coloured crusts, pustules; may have fever | CRP ↑, swab grows Staph | Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS × 7 days; if MRSA → clindamycin |
| **Eczema herpeticum (Kaposi varicelliform eruption)** | Widespread painful, punched‑out vesicles (roof often absent), fever, lymphadenopathy; can involve any skin | Malaise, eye involvement (conjunctivitis, keratitis), rapid spread over hours to days | IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h; dermatology/ophthalmology review; barrier nursing |
| **Erythroderma** (total body > 90% involved) | Red, scaly, hot, shivering, fluid loss, high‑output cardiac failure, hypoalbuminaemia | Fever, tachycardia, peripheral oedema, generalised lymphadenopathy | Urgent admission, IV fluids, emollients, topical mild steroids, dermatology review; rule out lymphoma |
| **Lichenification (chronic)** | Thickened, leathery skin with exaggerated skin markings; persists even after inflammation settles | Hyperpigmentation in darker skin types | Topical potent steroid pulse; need patient education to stop scratching |
| **Growth retardation (paediatric)** | Falling off growth centiles, delayed puberty | Height/weight < 2nd centile, bone age delay | Optimise skin care, consider systemic therapy (e.g., ciclosporin), refer to paediatric endocrinology |
| **Psychosocial** | Poor self‑image, school absence, social withdrawal | Sleep disturbance, depression/anxiety screens positive | Counselling, support groups, treat itch aggressively; refer to mental health |

**Late‑presentation patterns (exam stems):**  
- A child with untreated severe atopic eczema develops sudden widespread weepy vesicles and high fever — *that’s eczema herpeticum*.  
- An adult with long‑standing eczema presents with exfoliative dermatitis, generalised lymphadenopathy, and weight loss — *could be erythrodermic lymphoma (Sézary syndrome) — always biopsy.*  
- A patient with moderate eczema who over‑uses potent steroids on the face presents with perioral dermatitis and steroid withdrawal erythema — *manage with antibiotic creams (metronidazole) and taper steroid.*

### 9. High‑Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**
- **S.A.F.E. Management** (initial approach):  
  - **S** — Soap substitutes (emollient cleanser)  
  - **A** — Anti‑inflammatory (topical steroid)  
  - **F** — Frequent emollient (moisturise 2–3 times daily)  
  - **E** — Education (itch‑scratch, trigger avoidance)
- **DRY SKIN** (typical findings):  
  - **D** Dryness (xerosis)  
  - **R** Redness (erythema)  
  - **Y** You itch (pruritus)  
  - **S** Scaling (fine, not silvery)  
  - **K** Keep it in the flexures  
  - **I** It’s chronic  
  - **N** No silvery scale (vs psoriasis)

**Clinical pearls (pattern‑recognition cues):**
1. **“Somersault sign”** in infants: The baby rubs face on the bed → excoriation on cheeks and chin; think atopic eczema first.
2. **Flexural lichenification in an adult with no previous history** → consider contact dermatitis (e.g., nickel allergy from belt buckle) or atopic eczema that was mild in childhood.
3. **Pustules on a background of eczema** = infected until proven otherwise — swab and treat.
4. **“Eyelid eczema”** in an adult female → patch test for cosmetics (especially thioglycolates in hair dye) as well as atopic.
5. **Palmar / plantar hyperlinearity** + eczema in a child → think filaggrin mutation — ask about ichthyosis vulgaris.
6. **Erythroderma + personal history of eczema** → still needs biopsy: Sézary syndrome can mimic severe atopic eczema.
7. **“Weekend therapy” (twice‑weekly proactive steroid)** reduces flares by 50% in moderate eczema — always teach this.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Mistaking nummular eczema for tinea corporis — both have annular plaques, but tinea has raised scaly border and central clearing; scraping for KOH.
- Using **aqueous cream** as a leave‑on moisturiser — it contains sodium lauryl sulphate (irritant) and can sting; better as a soap substitute.
- Forgetting to treat **Staph aureus** when exposed dermatitis is present — systemic antibiotics not always needed (bacterial load reduced by steroid + antibiotics in ointment? But if weeping, give oral).
- Prescribing **sedating antihistamines** daytime — causes sedation; use non‑sedating (cetirizine, loratadine) for day, sedating at night.
- Starting **oral steroids** without a plan for steroid‑sparing agent — rebound flare is almost guaranteed.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call senior (dermatology registrar or consultant):**
- Suspected **eczema herpeticum** — needs IV aciclovir and expert review.
- **Erythroderma** (widespread redness, scaling, systemic symptoms) — needs admission, skin biopsy, and ruling out lymphoma.
- **Severe facial eczema with periorbital involvement** — risk of eye involvement (keratitis, cataracts from steroids); ophthalmology review.
- **Failure of topical therapy** (potent steroid + emollient + wet wraps for 5–7 days no response) — need phototherapy or systemic agents.
- **Severe childhood eczema with growth failure** — paediatric dermatology and endocrinology input.
- **Worsening on systemic immunosuppressants** (e.g., rising creatinine on ciclosporin, leucopenia on azathioprine) — urgent dose adjustment / cessation.

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- **Sepsis** secondary to infected eczema (fever, hypotension, tachycardia, lactate > 2 mmol/L, altered mental state) — start IV antibiotics and fluids; consult ICU for vasopressors if septic shock.
- **Eczema herpeticum with disseminated herpes** (pneumonitis, hepatitis, encephalitis) — ICU for organ support.
- **Anaphylaxis** (rare — from topical antibiotic/NSAID allergy in a patient with eczema) — IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg, call crash team.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (or any single parameter scoring 3): escalate to senior clinician.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** (sepsis): start IV antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, consider ICU.
- **GCS
